children.name: Ortalis
children.description: Medium-sized, long-tailed cracids with slender builds and a raucous, far-carrying call that gives them their onomatopoeic name. They are among the most widespread and adaptable members of the family.

children.name: Ortalis vetula
children.description: A drab brown bird with a long tail and a pale throat, ranging from southern Texas through Central America. True to its name, it lacks the bright adornments of its relatives but makes up for it with a loud, scratchy 'cha-cha-lac' duet at dawn. It is a common resident of thorn forests and secondary scrub.

children.name: Ortalis ruficauda
children.description: Found in northern Colombia and Venezuela as well as Trinidad and Tobago, this chachalaca is distinguished by its chestnut-red undertail and tail tip. It inhabits dry and moist forests, often foraging in small flocks in the canopy. The species is the national bird of Trinidad and Tobago.

children.name: Crax
children.description: Large, heavy-bodied curassows with a prominent curled crest of feathers and brightly coloured bills and ceres. Males are predominantly black while females are often barred or rufous.

children.name: Crax rubra
children.description: One of the largest cracids, males reaching nearly one metre in length with glossy black plumage, a white belly and a bright yellow knob on the bill. It ranges from southern Mexico to western Ecuador in humid lowland forests. Females are chestnut-brown with heavily barred crests and wings.

children.name: Pauxi
children.description: Easily identified by the distinctive, bulbous casque on the forehead, which is hard and bone-like in texture. These large, terrestrial birds inhabit montane and foothill forests of South America.

children.name: Pauxi pauxi
children.description: Named for the extraordinary blue-grey helmet-like casque that covers its forehead and extends forward over the bill. It inhabits the cloud forests of the Colombian and Venezuelan Andes at elevations of 800 to 2,000 metres. Like all curassows, it is primarily frugivorous and spends most of its time on the forest floor.

children.name: Chamaepetes
children.description: Mid-sized, slender guans with a glossy black plumage and bare blue facial skin. They are restricted to montane forests of Central America and the northern Andes.

children.name: Chamaepetes unicolor
children.description: A uniformly glossy black guan with a blue face and bright red legs, endemic to the highland forests of Costa Rica and western Panama. It is a shy inhabitant of the cloud forest canopy, feeding on fruits and occasionally visiting mineral licks. Its loud, yelping alarm call is a hallmark of the Talamancan highlands.

children.name: Penelope
children.description: Medium-to-large guans with a bare throat wattle and a dark brown or blackish plumage with subtle white streaking on the neck and breast. They are arboreal and highly vocal.

children.name: Penelope purpurascens
children.description: A large, dull-brown guan with a shaggy crest, a red throat wattle and fine white streaking on the neck. Ranging from northern Mexico to western Ecuador, it inhabits humid lowland and montane forests. It travels in small flocks through the canopy, emitting loud, honking calls that carry for long distances.
